The individuals leave the discussion that training the dog with treats is amplified

cards.topic: Identity, Personality & Biological Bases of Behavior
cards.question_text: Groupthink is [...]
cards.answer_text: Groupthink is when maintaining harmony among group members is more important than analyzing the problem at hand. Usually in insulated groups with respected leaders. Ex: neighborhood people decide to meet to discuss a dog exhibiting bad behavior. Leader says the dog should be put down to avoid damage to the neighborhood. Instead of arguing with the leader and having a conflict, the neighbors agree that the dog should be put down

cards.topic: Identity, Personality & Biological Bases of Behavior
cards.question_text: The stanford prison experiment highlights [...] , [...] , [...] , and [...]
cards.answer_text: The stanford prison experiment highlights situational attribution , deindividualization , cognitive dissonance , and internalization situational attribution: situations can influence our behavior deindividualization: easy for the "guards" to treat prisoners like shit if they suffer from deindividualization. More or less, the guards saw them all as like this cohesive group that was just adressed individually by numbers, so they didnt feel bad cognitive dissonance: guards knew behavior was super shitty so they tried to overly justify behaviors as if the prisoners deserved what they were getting internalization: participants internalized their roles and let it influence their attitudes, cognitions, and behaviors sorry for the long card idk how else to explain this stuff in multiple cards

fields.Text: The stanford prison experiment highlights {{c1::situational attribution}}, {{c1::deindividualization}}, {{c1::cognitive dissonance}}, and {{c1::internalization}}
fields.Extra:
situational attribution: situations can influence our behavior

deindividualization: easy for the "guards" to treat prisoners like shit if they suffer from deindividualization. More or less, the guards saw them all as like this cohesive group that was just adressed individually by numbers, so they didnt feel bad

cognitive dissonance: guards knew behavior was super shitty so they tried to overly justify behaviors as if the prisoners deserved what they were getting

internalization: participants internalized their roles and let it influence their attitudes, cognitions, and behaviors

cards.topic: Social Processes & Socialization
cards.question_text: [Theory] states that deviance is a learned behavior that results from continuous exposure to others whom violate norms/laws .
cards.answer_text: The Theory of Differential Association states that deviance is a learned behavior that results from continuous exposure to others whom violate norms/laws . Ex: elite athlete who grows up believing that cheating is wrong and to be a successful athlete one must train hard, avoid drugs and alcohol, and be respectful to opponents. Elite athlete now switches teams and now new team member believe that using steroids, partying, and heckling is the best way to be successful at a sport. Perhaps even show athlete how to be deviant by demonstrating how to take a particular drug, introducing athlete to steroid, etc. Overtime the athlete will learn from the new team members that these behaviors (partying, heckling, and drug use) are acceptable even though they were not before. Accepts these new deviant behaviors overtime

cards.question_text: [Theory] states that a behavior is deviant if people have judged the behavior and labelled it as deviant .
cards.answer_text: Labeling theory states that a behavior is deviant if people have judged the behavior and labelled it as deviant . Deviance depends entirely on what's acceptable in society ex: Steroids can be labeled as deviant. They are not labeled as right or wrong. Steroids in professional sports can be labeled wrong as it is unfair and deviant. However, old men on steroids to ramp up testosterone and what not is not seen as being as bad.

cards.answer_text: Secondary deviance is deviance that has more serious consequences and has a severe negative reaction that produces a stigmatizing label that results in more deviant behavior example: Athlete uses steroids and his teammates label him deviant and exclude him from practice and call him a terrible player. This reaction leads him to use more steroids to be a better player and might lead to more dangerous steroids. Repeated deviance gives him a reputation of deviance and a stigma that stays with him for the rest of his career

cards.question_text: Secondary deviance is deviance that [...] and [...]
cards.answer_text: Secondary deviance is deviance that has more serious consequences and has a severe negative reaction that produces a stigmatizing label that results in more deviant behavior example: Athlete uses steroids and his teammates label him deviant and exclude him from practice and call him a terrible player. This reaction leads him to use more steroids to be a better player and might lead to more dangerous steroids. Repeated deviance gives him a reputation of deviance and a stigma that stays with him for the rest of his career

cards.topic: Social Processes & Socialization
cards.question_text: Strain theory states that [...]
cards.answer_text: Strain theory states that if an individual is blocked from attaining a culturally accepted goal, they may become frustrated/strained and turn to deviance This includes individuals who may not have legitimate means or ways to achieve success. Opportunity to be successful is NOT made equal to everyone. Ex: athlete attends a school that doesn’t have proper baseball training equipment or no coach, or funding. Athlete becomes frustrated and turns to deviant behavior. School lacks the resources, so athlete tries steroids to level the playing field.

cards.topic: Social Processes & Socialization
cards.question_text: Collective behavior is different from group behavior because; 1. [...] 2. [...] 3. [...]
cards.answer_text: Collective behavior is different from group behavior because; 1. collective behavior is time limited while groups last longer 2. collectives can be open while groups can be exclusive 3. collectives have loose norms while groups have strong/well-defined norms

cards.topic: Social Processes & Socialization
cards.question_text: Mass hysteria is [...]
cards.answer_text: Mass hysteria is when a large number of people experience unmanageable delusions and anxiety at the same time reaction spreads very quickly aand can reach more people through rumors and fears example: Tell people that there is a hurricane coming and this induces fear/anxiety in large numbers of people and then they rush to the supermarket to stock up and drive erratically and it all becomes a distaster

cards.topic: Social Processes & Socialization
cards.question_text: Mass psychogenic illness AKA epidemic hysteria is [...]
cards.answer_text: Mass psychogenic illness AKA epidemic hysteria is mass hysteria that can be a result of psychology where large amounts of individuals believe they have the same illness despite lack of disease Ex. after anthrax attack in US, after reports there were over 2000 false alarms. Individuals reported false symptoms of anthrax infection and because they believed they were exposed (which induced false symptoms).

cards.question_text: Riots are characterized by [...]
cards.answer_text: Riots are characterized by large number of people engaging in dangerous behavior, such as vandalism/violence/etc, usually as a result of a perceived issue like working conditions, race conflicts, etc Cause of the riot may be legitimate, but the group acts out in ways that are illegal/damaging to society
